segments.s535-g1: پراندیشه is a predicative adjective 'pensive/full of thought' placed before the copula; بود is the past-tense copula 'was'. آن شب is a temporal adverbial 'that night' (postposed here after the verb phrase, a common poetic order). از + کرم + شاه is a prepositional phrase 'because of / concerning the king's generosity' where از marks the cause/concern and کرم (generosity) is possessed by شاه (king).
segments.s535-g2: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بنشست is the simple past 'sat/took its seat' (intransitive) referring to خورشید (the subject) which follows here for emphasis; بر is the preposition 'upon/on' and جایگاه is 'place/seat'. The clause describes a coincident time: 'when the sun sat upon its place.'
segments.s535-g3: سپه 'host/army' is the subject; برگرفت is a past-tense verbal compound meaning 'took up / rose / moved off' (here 'moved off' from a position); از + لب آبگیر is a locative phrase 'from the brink/edge of the pond' with لب used in the sense 'brink/edge' and آبگیر 'pond/reservoir'.
segments.s535-g4: سوی is the preposition 'toward'; پارس is the place-name 'Pars (Persia)'; آمد is the past 'came/advanced'; دمان literally means 'hem/skirt' but in poetry names the trailing retinue or train (here 'retinue/entourage'); اردشیر is the proper name 'Ardashir'. Word order is poetically inverted: the motion verb آمد governs the following noun phrase دمان اردشیر.
segments.s535-g5: پس is the temporal/consecutive 'then'; لشکر او is 'his army/host' (لشکر = army, او = his); بیامد is the past 'came' (here announcing arrival); سپاه is an appositive or reinforcing noun 'troops/host' placed after the verb phrase in poetic word order.
segments.s535-g6: ز هر سو is a adverbial phrase 'from every side/direction' (ز used poetically for 'from'); گرفتند is the past transitive 'they seized/took' and بر شاه‌راه is the object phrase 'upon the royal road/highway' (بر = on/upon, شاه‌راه = main/royal road). The verb governs the prepositional object in inverted poetic order.
segments.s535-g7: بکشتند is the past plural 'they killed'; هرکس که introduces a free relative 'whoever / anyone who'; بد نامدار is a compound where بد 'bad' negates or pejoratively modifies نامدار 'renowned' to yield 'ill-reputed / infamous'. The clause means 'they slew anyone who was ill-reputed.'
segments.s535-g8: همی is an adverb marking continuous or habitual action ('continually / kept on'); تاختند is past plural 'they charged/rode/galloped' (here a martial motion 'charged'); از پس is a prepositional phrase 'after/in the wake of' (از + پس together), and شهریار is 'the king/sovereign'. Together: 'they kept charging after the king.'
segments.s535-g81: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/through' marking cause; گفتار ایشان is the noun phrase 'their speech/words' (ایشان = 'they/them'). دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' with the enclitic ش = 'his', and گشت شاد is a compound predication: گشت (past of گشتن) = 'became/turned' + شاد = 'glad'. Word order here is cause phrase → subject → verb → predicative adjective.
segments.s535-g82: همی رفت is an older/poetic imperfective/continuous past 'he continued/went on' (همی adds durative or iterative force). پیروز here is an adjective 'victorious' modifying an implicit subject (he). The coordination و links the following verbless verblike clause دل پر ز داد: دل پر (دل + adjective پر = 'heart full') with ز داد 'from/of justice' where داد is the noun 'justice'. Overall the clause says he proceeded in a victorious mood, his heart full of justice.
segments.s535-g83: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. برداشت is used in a verbal sense 'took up / removed / set out' (here 'departed' or 'took himself away'); زانجا = 'from there' (thence). جهاندار شاه is a doubled-title construction 'the world-ruling king' (جهاندار + شاه), with the titles stacked before/after as is common in epic diction.
segments.s535-g84: جوانان 'the young men' is the subject plural, and برفتند is the past plural 'they went/they departed'. با او 'with him' uses با + pronoun او. به راه is a directional phrase 'on the road/towards the way' (به + راه). The verb precedes the oblique phrase here because Persian allows flexible order; overall: the young men accompanied him.
segments.s535-g85: همی رفت again gives a durative/iterative past 'he went on / continued'. روشن‌دل و یادگیر are two coordinated adjectival/epithet phrases: روشن‌دل 'light-/clear‑hearted' (cheerful, bright‑minded) and یادگیر 'one who takes to learning' (apt to learn). They function as manner descriptions of how he went.
segments.s535-g86: سرافراز is an adjectival predicate 'triumphant / high‑honored'. تا is the spatial/temporal preposition 'until / as far as' marking extent, and خوره اردشیر names the destination/place; اردشیر is the proper name 'Ardashir'. The segment reads 'triumphant as far as Khurreh‑Ardashir.' (If خوره is a toponym here, it functions as a place name rather than its literal lexical senses.)
segments.s535-g87: چو = 'when'. The first بر before شاه functions 'against' (an army coming upon the king), so the phrase means 'when an army rose up against the king'. The second بر with شد (شد = 'happened/rose') combines to give 'an army came upon/the army rose' (سپاه is the noun 'army/host'). انجمن is then the following noun 'assembly' — the line sets the scene: with an army against the king, an assembly (of people) occurred.
segments.s535-g88: بزرگان فرزانه و رای‌زن is a coordinated nominal phrase describing the members of that assembly: بزرگان 'the great/elders', فرزانه 'wise/sage', and رای‌زن 'counselors/advisers' (رای‌زن is an agent noun 'those who give counsel'). The adjectives/nouns are appositive epithets enumerating the assembly's character.
segments.s535-g65: یِکی (one/certain) + جای دارند: the phrase functions existentially — literally 'they have a place' but here means 'there is a place' or 'a certain place exists'. دارَند is 3rd-person plural present used in older/poetic Persian for possession/existence. بر + تیغ کوه = a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the mountain's edge/ridge' (تیغ = point/edge, کوه = mountain), placed after the verb phrase as is common in classical ordering.
segments.s535-g66: بدو is a clitic-like prepositional form 'to it/unto it' referring back to the place just mentioned. اندرون 'within/inside' marks location; the following nouns کرم و گنج و گروه are listed as contents located there. The sequence uses simple coordination (و) to list the things contained inside the place.
segments.s535-g67: به پیش is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in front' or 'ahead'. اندرون شهر 'the inner part of the city' is the thing placed in front; و دریا بپشت links with 'and the sea behind' (بپشت is an older/compacted particle meaning 'at the back/behind'). Word order is topical: location first (in front), then what is located there.
segments.s535-g68: دژی (a fortress) is presented as another salient feature; بر سر کوه is a prepositional phrase 'on the mountain's summit' (سر = top/summit). و راهی درشت links a second feature: راهی here denotes a road or way, qualified by درشت 'large/broad'. The clause lists landscape/fortification elements in parallel.
segments.s535-g69: همان کرم = 'that same/that very generosity' (همان stresses identity). کز is the relative particle 'که + ز' = 'which/from which', introducing the relative clause مغز آهرمنست 'its core is Ahriman' (مغز = core, آهرمنست = 'is Ahriman' / 'belongs to Ahriman'). The clause attributes the origin of this 'karm' to Ahriman.
segments.s535-g70: جهان آفریننده را is the object/topic phrase 'the world's Creator' (جهان = world; آفریننده = creator; را marks the definite object/emphatic topic). دشمنست is the copula+predicate 'is an enemy' (classical fused form). The whole clause states the hostile relation: that generosity is hostile to the Creator of the world.
segments.s535-g71: همی is an adverb meaning 'constantly/continually' in older Persian. کرم خوانی is a verbal construction: خوانی (from خواندن) here means 'is read/recited/sung' or 'is celebrated' — the noun کرم is the theme being proclaimed. به + چرم اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/within the leather interior' (چرم likely refers to a leather-lined inner chamber or curtain); the preposition sequence indicates where the action takes place.
segments.s535-g72: یکی = 'a certain/one' introduces an indefinite subject. دیو 'demon' is the noun; جنگیست is a predicate 'is warlike' (جنگی + -ست). ریزنده خون is a nominative/epithet phrase 'a shedder/scatterer of blood' (ریزنده from ریختن 'to pour/let fall', خون = blood), describing the demon's violent character.
segments.s535-g25: که is a relative pronoun here ('who/that') introducing a relative clause. بگریخت is an old poetic past (3rd sg.) meaning 'he/it fled' (the prefixed ب- is a classical/poetic marker). از introduces the source 'from', and وز here functions as a compressed poetic conjunction meaning 'and from' linking two sources (Karam and Haftvād). کرم is used as a proper name in this context rather than its common noun senses.
segments.s535-g26: وزان ('and from that / from them') continues the trace back to those people. بی‌هنر modifies the following noun phrase and means 'unskilled/unrefined'. لشکر بدنژاد is a nominal phrase 'an army/host (لشکر) of bad/low birth (بدنژاد)'; note the adjective بدنژاد follows لشکر, a poetic word order that emphasizes the quality.
segments.s535-g27: بجستند is a past plural verb 'they leapt/sprang'. از جای is a prepositional phrase 'from the place/position'. هر دو جوان ('each of the two youths' = both young men) is the (understood) subject; Persian permits the verb to appear before the subject here, a common poetic order.
segments.s535-g28: پر از درد is an adjectival phrase 'full of pain' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of/from', درد = 'sorrow/pain'). گشتند is the past plural of 'to become' so the clause means 'they became full of pain'. و links the next predicate, and تیره‌روان is a descriptive adjective meaning 'gloomy/dark‑faced' (used adverbially/attributively here).
segments.s535-g29: فرود آوریدندش combines فرود (descent/alighting) with the verb آوردند (they brought) plus the enclitic object -ش 'him' (آوریدندش = 'they brought him down'). از پشت زین is a locative phrase 'from the back of the saddle' (پشت = back/rear, زین = saddle). The whole clause is a simple past action 'they dismounted him / brought him down.'
segments.s535-g30: بران is a poetic connective meaning 'thereupon' / 'on that'. مهتران here denotes the chiefs/leading men (the provided lemma/senses list does not match; this is a lexical/practical identification). خواندند is past plural 'they cried/called' and its object is آفرین — the exclamation 'Bravo' or 'Well done'. The structure is 'thereupon the chiefs cried
segments.s535-g31: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain' (here 'they set up a certain'). جای خرم is a noun plus adjective 'a joyful/pleasant place' (خرم = joyous/verdant). بپیراستند is a past plural poetic form 'they adorned / they set up' (the b- prefix is classical/poetic). The clause means 'they made/arranged a pleasant place.'
segments.s535-g32: پسندیده is an adjective 'pleasing/approved'. خوانی here denotes a banquet/feast or the act/place of feasting (derived from خوان 'table/feast'); the provided senses for خوانی were verbal and do not capture this nominal sense, so it is treated as a noun 'feast/entertainment'. بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arranged' (again the poetic b-/bi- prefix with past plural ending). The phrase means 'they prepared/trimmed a pleasing feast/setting.'
segments.s535-g97: به here introduces an instrument ('with/by'); شمشیر هندی is a noun + adjectival epithet ('Indian sword') placed after the noun in classical style. بزد is an archaic past 3rd-person singular verb 'he struck/ smote'. گردنش is the direct object 'his neck' (گردن + enclitic -ش = 'his').
segments.s535-g98: به آتش در انداخت is a verbal phrase: به + آتش ('towards/into the fire') + در ('in/into') + انداخت (past 'threw/ cast'). بی‌سر تنش is a noun phrase 'his headless body' (بی‌سر 'headless' + تن 'body' + enclitic -ش 'his') and functions as the object of انداخت.
segments.s535-g99: هرانکس is an indefinite relative 'whoever/whosoever'. کزان 'from which/ of that' ties this person to the preceding 'seed/offspring' (تخمه). آمد is a past verb 'came/appeared'. به مشت is an instrumental phrase 'with a fist' (به + مشت), indicating how he struck them.
segments.s535-g100: به خنجر 'with the dagger' (instrumental به). هم = 'also' or 'even'. اندر زمانش = 'in/at the same moment' (اندر = in/within; زمانش = moment/time, with -ش 'his/their'). بکشت is an archaic past-form verbal form meaning here 'killed/slaughtered' (epic past); the object is omitted but understood from the previous clause.
segments.s535-g101: مگر here means 'except'. دختری is an indefinite 'a girl/maiden'. کان is an archaic relative particle ('who/that') linking to the relative clause. نهان گشت is a verbal phrase 'went into hiding' (نهان 'hidden' + گشت 'became/went'), and زوی ('ز وی') functions as a prepositional-like element 'from her / herself' marking the agent/ source in this idiom.
segments.s535-g102: همه شهر 'the whole city' is the subject. ازو = 'of/for her' (from her) marks the person sought. گشت here = 'became' (so the city 'became' something). پر + جست و جوی is an adjectival/nominal phrase 'full of searching' (پر 'full (of)'; جست (here 'seek') + و + جوی 'search' form the compound sense 'searching/quest'), so the line reads 'the whole city became full of search for her.'
segments.s535-g89: This clause uses the past participle/pret. برآسود (lit. 'was at ease') to mean 'he rested' or 'was at peace.' یک چند (literally 'one few') is a poetic way to say 'for a short time / for some days'; و links this temporal clause to the following event. روزی functions as a temporal adverb ('one day' — literally 'a day appeared'), and the preposition به introduces the object (داد) of the coming verb which appears in the next segment, so the verb of the clause is postponed to the following segment.
segments.s535-g90: بیامد is a simple past verb 'he came.' سوی is a directional preposition 'toward'/'to.' مهرک نوش‌زاد here is a proper-name phrase (M-e-h-r-a-k + epithet Nushzad); the object of آمدن is the person toward whom he came. This completes the idiom begun in the previous segment: 'روزی به داد بیامد' = 'one day came to the help.'
segments.s535-g91: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' مهرک is the subject (proper name). بیارست is a past verb meaning 'he prepared / made ready,' and it is followed by the infinitive رفتن ('to go'), so the structure is 'when Mehrak made ready to go' (prepared to set out). به جنگ is the goal/charge 'to war' or 'for battle.'
segments.s535-g92: کرد is the past of 'to do/make' and here takes جهان ('the world') as its object: literally 'he made the world.' بر خویشتن ('upon himself') is a prepositional phrase marking the target of that action (he imposed it on himself). تار و تنگ is a paired adjective phrase meaning 'constricted / narrow and tight'; the verb+object+prepositional phrase thus yields 'he made the world narrow for himself.'
segments.s535-g93: This is a poetic word order: به جهرم ('to Jahrom') gives the destination, چو ('when') introduces the temporal clause, نزدیک شد is the intransitive past 'approached / came near,' and پادشا is the subject 'the king.' The verb (شد) follows the adverbial first, a common inversion in verse.
segments.s535-g94: نهان گشت is a compound past meaning 'became hidden' or 'hid.' ز (here contracted with the pronoun that follows in speech: ز + او = زو) means 'from,' so the phrase means 'hid from him.' مهرک بی‌وفا is the subject phrase 'Mehrak the unfaithful,' placed after the verb (verb–subject order is common in Persian poetry).
segments.s535-g95: دل پادشا is a genitive 'the king's heart' (noun + possessor). پر ز پیکار شد is a predicate: پر 'full (of)' takes the preposition ز ('of') plus پیکار ('battle/fighting'), and شد is the past copula/verb 'became' — together 'the king's heart became full of battle/fighting.'
segments.s535-g96: همی بود is a durative past construction meaning 'it continued to be so' or 'it remained.' تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until,' and او گرفتار شد is a passive/result clause 'he was captured / became taken.' The sequence means 'it remained so until he was taken captive.'
segments.s535-g33: نشستند is a 3rd‑person plural past tense (‘they sat [down]’); here it begins the clause. با شاه گردان is a prepositional phrase meaning “with the king and (his) retinue” (با = 'with'); به خوان is another prepositional phrase (‘at/to the banquet/table’) placed after the group. Word order is verb → accompaniment → place.
segments.s535-g34: پرستش گرفتند is a common construction where the object (پرستش ‘worship’) precedes the past verb گرفتند (‘they took up / they did’), meaning ‘they began/engaged in worship.’ هر دو جوان is a nominative phrase ‘both young men’ with هر دو (each/two together) before the noun.
segments.s535-g35: به آواز is an adverbial phrase ‘in a voice / aloud’ (به + noun used adverbially). گفتند is the 3rd‑person plural past of گفتن (‘they said’); کای is the vocative particle ‘O’; سرفراز is an epithet addressed in the vocative (‘exalted / victorious one’).
segments.s535-g36: غم و شادمانی is a coordinated noun phrase ‘sorrow and joy.’ نماند is the negative past form (ن + ماند) meaning ‘did not remain’ or more idiomatically ‘does not last’; دراز is an adverbial adjective ‘long.’ Together: ‘sorrow and joy do not remain long.’
segments.s535-g37: نگه کن is an imperative combination: نگه (a noun/verb‑stem ‘look, gaze’) plus کن (imperative ‘do’) → ‘look!’ یا که introduces the following content-clause; ضحاک is the proper name Zahhak; بیدادگر is an epithet meaning ‘tyrant/cruel one.’
segments.s535-g38: چه is an interrogative/exclamative ‘what.’ آورد is past tense ‘brought/caused’ (here: ‘brought about / inflicted’). زان = زِ + آن ‘from that’ (referring to that throne), تخت شاهی = ‘royal throne’ (تخت ‘throne’, شاهی ‘royal’). به سر is a locative/figurative phrase ‘upon the head/of’ — together: ‘what has he brought upon that royal throne.’
segments.s535-g39: هم is an additive/adversative particle ‘also / even.’ افراسیاب is the proper name Afrasiab. آن before an adjective is a demonstrative ‘that’; بداندیش is an adjective ‘ill‑minded / malicious’; مرد is the noun ‘man.’ The phrase is an appositive: ‘also Afrasiab, that ill‑minded man.’
segments.s535-g40: کزو is a relative/prepositional particle ‘from whom / by whom’ referring back to that man. بد دل is a compound adjective ‘ill‑hearted / malicious’ modifying شهریاران (the rulers); به درد is a prepositional phrase ‘to/into pain’ meaning ‘caused grief.’ The whole clause thus says that by him the rulers’ hearts were afflicted.
segments.s535-g9: خروش آمد از پس is a simple past narrative clause: خروش (a cry/clamor) is the subject and آمد (came/rose) is the past tense verb. از پس here means "from behind/after" (a spatial/temporal phrase). که‌ای بخت کرم is an archaic/exclamatory vocative phrase: که‌ای is a poetic/intensifying form introducing an address, and بخت کرم (literally “fortune/good luck, generosity”) is being vocatively called out.
segments.s535-g10: که introduces the following optative/exclamatory clause. رخشنده (radiant/bright) modifies an implied noun; بادا is an old optative particle meaning “may it be”. سر از تخت کرم is a figurative phrase: سر (the head/top/exaltation) + از تخت (from/on the throne) + کرم (generosity/bounty). The whole clause is a wish: "May your radiant head be raised/exalted on the throne of generosity."
segments.s535-g11: همی is a frequentative/progressive adverb meaning "continually/kept on"; هرکسی is the subject meaning "everyone/each person." گفت is the past-tense verb "said," and کاینت شگفت is a quoted/exclamatory nominal phrase (کاینت = this/that; شگفت = wonderful/marvel), functioning as what people were saying.
segments.s535-g12: کزین is a prepositional compound meaning "from this/therefrom." هرکس here is general "each person/anyone." اندازه باید گرفت is an impersonal necessity/obligation structure: اندازه (measure/limit/lesson) + باید (it is necessary) + گرفت (to take). Literarily: "one must take measure/learn a lesson from these people."
segments.s535-g13: بیامد is past tense "he came." گریزان is a predicative adjective/participial adjective meaning "fleeing/flighty," linked to the subject. و connects the next phrase: دل پر نهیب is a noun phrase where دل (heart) is modified by a compound adjective پر نهیب (full of نهیب), meaning "filled with a thunderous cry/alarm" or "full of alarm." The cluster describes his state as he arrived.
segments.s535-g14: همی is the frequentative adverb again (kept on); تاخت is the past tense verb "galloped/rushed." اندر introduces a locative phrase; فراز و نشیب is a paired noun phrase meaning "ups and downs" or "heights and descents." Together: "he kept rushing in/through the ups and downs."
segments.s535-g15: یکی is an indefinite marker "a certain" or "one" introducing an item; شارستان is the noun "city/town." دید is the past-tense verb "he saw/beheld." جایی بزرگ is an appositive noun phrase (جایی = a place/somewhere, بزرگ = great/large) further describing the city. Word order places the descriptive phrase after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s535-g16: ازان سو is a locative phrase meaning "from that side/thereupon" (ازان = from that; سو = side). براندند is 3rd-person plural past "they drove/charged/advanced." گردان is a collective noun "troop/unit." چو is the simile word "like/as," and گرگ is the comparison "wolves." The clause describes a troop charging from that side like wolves.
segments.s535-g49: خوش آمدش is a verbal phrase meaning “it pleased him”: خوش = ‘pleasant’ and آمد with the enclitic -ش = ‘to him’ (past). گفتار آن دلنواز is the noun phrase ‘the speech/words of that heart-pleasing one’ and functions as the thing that pleased him; word order (verb phrase before its logical noun phrase) is typical of classical Persian poetic style.
segments.s535-g50: Two past verbs are joined by و and share the object راز: بکرد آشکارا = ‘made [it] clear/open’ (with آشکارا as adverb) and بنمود راز = archaic/poetic past ‘he showed/revealed the secret’; the b‑prefix in classical Persian verbs (بکرد, بنمود) is a poetic/paratactic past form.
segments.s535-g51: که introduces a clause of self-identification: فرزند ساسان منم اردشیر literally orders as ‘a son of Sasan am I, Ardashir.’ منم is the copular form ‘I am’ attached to من; اردشیر is in apposition (the proper name).
segments.s535-g52: تریتا word order: یکی پند = ‘a single/one piece of advice’ is the subject, باید is impersonal/modal ‘is necessary / I must have,’ مرا (here split in the data as م + را) = ‘for me’ (object/beneficiary), and دلپذیر qualifies the پند (‘pleasant’). In natural English: ‘I need one pleasant piece of counsel.’
segments.s535-g53: چه سازیم is a first‑person plural interrogative construction ‘what shall we do’; سازیم is present/future‑oriented ‘we do/will do.’ The repeated با before each item ('با کرم و با هفتواد') stresses ‘with Karam and with Haftvād’ (here هفتواد is a proper name).
segments.s535-g54: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause modifying the preceding person(s): نام و نژادش = ‘his name and his lineage’ (the possessive -ش attaches), به گیتی مباد is a jussive/wish formula ‘may it not be in the world’ (مباد = ‘may it not be’), i.e. a curse or wish that his name/lineage cease to exist publicly.
segments.s535-g55: سپهبدار ایران is the title/subj. ‘the commander (general) of Iran.’ چو = ‘when,’ introducing a temporal clause; بگشاد (classical past of ‘open/reveal’) governs the object راز = ‘the secret’ — thus ‘when the commander of Iran opened/revealed the secret.’
segments.s535-g56: جوانانش = ‘his young men/young ones’ (جوانان + possessive -ش). بردند نماز is an idiomatic past construction ‘they took/held prayer’ = ‘they performed prayer’; هر دو here functions as ‘both’ (i.e. the two of them). Word order places verb before its object, as common in classical diction.
segments.s535-g57: بگفتند is the archaic simple past plural 'they said' (b- prefix + گفتند). هر دو follows it and means 'both' ('هر' = 'each/every' used with 'دو'). که introduces the reported content (a subordinate clause). نوشه بدی is a noun phrase: نوشه 'a draught/portion' modified by the following noun بدی 'evil' (genitive/attributive relation common in classical Persian).
segments.s535-g58: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. ز تو is the prepositional phrase 'from/of you' (poetic ז/از = from). دور modifies the following noun phrase دست بدی: here دور = 'distant/far' and دست بدی literally 'the hand/power of evil' (metaphorical), so the clause means 'may the hand (power) of evil be far from you' — the copular/optative verb (باد) is omitted in the line but implied.
segments.s535-g59: تن و جان ما is a coordinated noun phrase 'our body and soul' (ما is the possessor). پیش تو = 'in your presence' / 'before you'. بنده باد is a fixed optative formula: بنده 'servant' + باد (optative particle 'may it be'), i.e. 'may (they) be servants' — the verb is modal/optative, not a present indicative.
segments.s535-g60: همیشه 'always/ever' modifies the wish. روان تو is 'your soul/spirit' (possessive construction). پاینده باد repeats the optative pattern: پاینده 'everlasting' as predicate adjective + باد 'may', giving 'may (your spirit) be everlasting'.
segments.s535-g61: سخنها ('the words') is the head noun, followed by a relative/nominalizing که پرسیدی از ما 'which you asked of us' — که introduces the clause and پرسیدی is the (past) verb 'you asked' (classical second-person past). از ما 'of/from us' is the object-marking prepositional phrase. درست here is an adverb/adjective meaning 'correctly/properly' that will qualify the following verb (بگوییم).
segments.s535-g62: بگوییم is a hortatory/subjunctive first-person-plural form 'let us say / we should say' (optative/first-person subjunctive used to propose speaking). تا introduces purpose 'so that / in order that'. چاره سازی is a compound meaning 'making/bringing about a remedy/solution' (چاره = remedy; سازی = 'making' as a deverbal noun). نخست 'first' qualifies the action; the subject of چاره‌سازی is left implicit (contextually the addressee may act).
segments.s535-g63: تو در جنگ 'you in battle' is the locative phrase placing the addressee in the context. با کرم و با هفتواد 'with Kerm and with Haftvād' uses با twice for rhythm; کرم and هفتواد are opponents (here treated as names). Word order is standard: preposition + noun(s).
segments.s535-g64: This is a conditional/admonitory clause. بسنده نه‌ای‌گر is an archaic conditional phrase roughly 'if you are not sufficient' (بسنده = 'sufficient/enough'; نه‌ای‌گر functions like 'if not' or 'if you are not'). نپیچی is a negative present/subjunctive/imperative form 'do not turn' (ن‑ negative prefix + پیچی 'you turn'), addressed to the addressee. ز داد = 'from justice' (ز = from; داد = justice). Together: 'if you are not sufficient, do not turn away from justice.'
segments.s535-g41: This is a temporal/relative clause: 'سکندر که آمد برین روزگار' = 'When/which Alexander came in those days'. 'که' is a relative/temporal connector ('who/when') linking 'سکندر' to the verb 'آمد' (simple past). 'برین' is the fused preposition + demonstrative (بر + این) meaning 'in/at these/those', and 'روزگار' names the time/age.
segments.s535-g42: Main clause with a past transitive verb: 'بکشت' is past third-person singular 'he killed' (archaic prefixed form). 'آنک' is a relative pronoun 'that/which/who' introducing the object, and the following noun phrase 'بد در جهان شهریار' describes that object as 'an evil king in the world' (در + جهان = 'in the world').
segments.s535-g43: 'برفتند' is past plural 'they went/left.' 'ز ایشان' (written here as 'زیشان') means 'of/from them.' 'به جز' is the fixed expression 'except,' so 'به جز نام زشت' = 'except a bad/vile name' (i.e. only disgrace remained).
segments.s535-g44: 'نماند' is perfect/past 'did not remain' (nothing was left). 'و' links clauses. 'نیابند' is negative + present/subjunctive form of 'یافتن/یابیدن' here meaning 'they do not attain/achieve' (i.e. they do not obtain). 'خرم بهشت' = 'joyful/blissful paradise' (adjective + noun).
segments.s535-g45: 'نماند' again is 'did not remain/was not left.' 'همین نیز' = 'even this / this also.' 'بر هفتواد' uses the preposition 'بر' in the sense 'for/upon/with respect to Haftvād'—i.e. saying that this thing was not left to Haftvād.
segments.s535-g46: This clause contains the verb 'بپیچد', a present/subjunctive/optative form of 'پیچیدن' here meaning 'to turn/come to' (in the idiom 'بپیچد به فرجام' = 'come to an end/meet a fate'). The clause expresses what did not remain to happen: 'to (come) to the end/fate of this ill-born race.' 'به فرجام' = 'to the end/outcome', and 'این بدنژاد' = 'this ill‑born / ignoble stock.'
segments.s535-g47: 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from' or 'because of.' 'ز گفتار ایشان' = 'from/their speech' (i.e. 'because of what they said'). 'دل شهریار' is a noun phrase 'the heart of the king' (order: possessee after possessed in classical Persian without an ezāfe here).
segments.s535-g48: 'چنان ... شد' is a simile: 'چنان تازه شد' = 'became so fresh.' 'شد' is past 'became.' 'چون گل اندر بهار' = 'like a flower in spring,' with 'اندر' poetic for 'in.' The whole phrase compares the king's heart to a fresh spring flower.
segments.s535-g73: سخنها (direct object) is fronted; چو + past verb بشنید makes a temporal clause meaning “when he heard.” ز + او (written together here as زو) is the poetic/prepositional “from him” (ز = ‘from’ + او = ‘him’), so اردشیر is the person who heard; the verb بشنید is simple past (he heard).
segments.s535-g74: همه here functions adverbially (‘altogether/entirely’). مهر جوینده is a nominal/adjectival phrase: مهر (affection) + جوینده (seeking) = “seeking affection” or “affectionate.” The coordinating و links دلپذیر (‘pleasing to the heart’) as a second predicative description of the subject (Ardashir is described as both مهر‌جوینده and دلپذیر).
segments.s535-g75: بدیشان is a dative-directional phrase ‘to them’ (بد-یشان = ب + ایشان, here meaning ‘to/for them’); چنین گفت = ‘said thus/so’ (گفت is past ‘said’). The following ک introduces the content clause; آری + رواست functions as the predicate ‘it is fitting/allowed’ (رواست = روا + است).
segments.s535-g76: بد و نیک is a fixed pairing ‘bad and good’ (both sides). ایشان refers back to ‘them’/the youths. The sequence م را appears in the text-split here: this corresponds to the object/possessive idea (‘m را’ ~ مرا / ‘me’/‘mine’ in context) and با شماست places that relation ‘with you’ — syntactically this clause says their good and bad belong/are with you (and me). ست is the copula ‘is.’ Note: the tokenization here is irregular (م and را split); read together as the object/possessive sense in context.
segments.s535-g77: جوانان is the plural subject ‘the youths/young men.’ ورا (= و + را) here means ‘to him’ (dative/oblique). پاسخ is the noun ‘reply/answer’ and آراستند is past, 3rd-person plural ‘they prepared/arranged/adorned’ — here ‘they gave/prepared an answer.’
segments.s535-g78: دل هوشمندش is the object being affected: دل ‘heart’ modified by هوشمند (intelligent/aware) with the possessive suffix -ش ‘his.’ بپیراستند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb (ب + پیراستند) meaning ‘they adorned/arrayed’ (the B- prefix is the verbal stem form; here it carries the past action ‘they adorned/beautified’).
segments.s535-g79: که begins the content/explanatory clause ‘for/that.’ ما بندگانیم is present/copular ‘we are servants’ (بندگان + -یم). پیشت is an adverb ‘in your presence / before you.’ به پای is a formula meaning ‘at the feet of’ (an idiom of humility: ‘at your feet’).
segments.s535-g80: همیشه is the adverb ‘always.’ به نیکی is a prepositional phrase ‘in/with goodness’ (indicating the manner/aim). ترا here is the 2nd-person object form (ت + را) ‘you,’ and رهنمای functions as a predicative noun ‘(a) guide’ — the clause therefore expresses: ‘we (are) always, at your feet, [to be] a guide to you in goodness.’ The verb ‘to be’ is understood from the copula earlier.
segments.s535-g17: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic use). تنگ اندر آمد is literal word order 'narrow into came' — تنگ 'narrow' + اندر 'into/in' + آمد 'came' (past). یکی خانه دید follows: یکی 'a certain/one' + خانه 'house' + دید 'saw' — standard SOV in poetry but rendered naturally in English.
segments.s535-g18: به در بر is an adverbial phrase 'at/unto the door' with the prepositions stacked (به 'towards/into' + در 'door' + بر 'on/at') — poetic ordering. دو برنای بیگانه is 'two young strangers' (دو 'two' + برنای 'young one' + بیگانه 'stranger'), and دید is the past verb 'saw'. The verb appears at the end, consistent with Persian poetic word order.
segments.s535-g19: ببودند is an archaic/past plural 'they were' (past tense). بر در زمانی به پای is a stacked locative/adverbial phrase: بر در 'at the door', زمانی 'for a while', به پای 'at the foot/threshold' — the pieces specify duration and exact location before the verb in poetic order.
segments.s535-g20: بپرسید is a past tense form 'asked/inquired' (here rendered 'he asked'). زو is the fused poetic prepositional phrase از او 'of/from him' (given as separate tokens z + o in the apparatus). این دو پاکیزه‌رای is the object 'these two, of pure mien/clean-minded' (این دو 'these two' + پاکیزه‌رای 'pure-minded').
segments.s535-g21: که introduces an indirect question/relative 'who/that'. بیگه 'the other day/recently' + چنین 'thus/like this' set the temporal/qualifying frame. از کجا رفته‌اید is the interrogative clause 'from where have you gone/come?' with رفته‌اید second-person plural perfect/past ('you have gone/you came').
segments.s535-g22: که again links the following explanatory clause. با گرد here means 'with (a) dust' — i.e. 'covered in dust' (گرد as dust). راهید is an archaic verbal form referring to being on the road / travelling (the provided sense-labels don't exactly match modern usage, so sense is left unspecified); و links the clauses; آشفته‌اید is present/past-state 'you are disordered/dishevelled' (second-person plural).
segments.s535-g23: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' + گفت 'said'); زین سو گذشت اردشیر literally 'from this side passed Ardashir' — زین سو 'this side/this way' + گذشت 'passed' + اردشیر (proper name). Poetic order places the verb before the subject.
segments.s535-g24: ازو باز ماندیم = 'we remained/lagged behind from him' (ازو = 'from him', باز ماندیم = 'we remained back/lagged behind'); بر خیره خیر is an adjectival/idiomatic phrase 'in astonishment at the good' (بر 'upon/in' + خیره 'staring/astonished' + خیر 'good'), describing their stunned reaction.

sentences.s535-s1: That night he was pensive because of the king's generosity, when the sun took its place upon the seat.
sentences.s535-s2: The host rose from the brink of the pond; Ardashir's retinue moved toward Pars.
sentences.s535-s3: Then his host came; forces from every direction took possession of the royal road.
sentences.s535-s4: They killed anyone who was ill-reputed; they kept charging in the wake of the king.
sentences.s535-s41: From their words his heart became glad; he went on victorious, his heart full of justice.
sentences.s535-s42: When the sovereign king departed from there, the young men went with him on the road.
sentences.s535-s43: He continued on, bright‑hearted and quick to learn, triumphant up to Khurreh (Ardashir).
sentences.s535-s44: When an army rose against the king, there was an assembly of great, wise elders and counselors.
sentences.s535-s33: A certain place stands on the mountain's ridge; within it are generosity, treasure, and a company.
sentences.s535-s34: In front lies the city's interior, and behind it the sea; a fortress on the mountain's summit and a broad road.
sentences.s535-s35: That very generosity whose core is Ahriman is an enemy of the world's Creator.
sentences.s535-s36: That generosity is continually proclaimed inside the leather interior; there is one warlike demon, a shedder of blood.
sentences.s535-s13: who fled from Karam and from Haftvād, and from them came an unskilled, low‑born host.
sentences.s535-s14: Both young men sprang from their places; they were filled with pain and became gloomy.
sentences.s535-s15: They brought him down from the back of the saddle; then the chiefs cried out 'Bravo.'
sentences.s535-s16: They prepared a pleasant, joyful place; they arranged an agreeable feast (or entertainment).
sentences.s535-s49: He struck his neck with an Indian sword and cast his headless body into the fire.
sentences.s535-s50: Whoever of that offspring came, he struck (them) with his fist and also killed (them) with the dagger at the same moment.
sentences.s535-s51: Except for a girl who hid herself, the whole city became full of searching for her.
sentences.s535-s45: He rested for a short while, and one day came to the aid of Mehrak Nushzad.
sentences.s535-s46: When Mehrak prepared to go to war, he made the world narrow and tight for himself.
sentences.s535-s47: When the king drew near to Jahrom, the unfaithful Mehrak hid from him.
sentences.s535-s48: The king's heart became full of fighting; it continued so until he was captured.
sentences.s535-s17: They sat down at the banquet with the king and his retinue; both young men began to worship.
sentences.s535-s18: They cried aloud, "O exalted one, neither sorrow nor joy will long remain."
sentences.s535-s19: Look — Zahhak the tyrant: what has he brought upon that royal throne?
sentences.s535-s20: Also Afrasiab, that ill‑minded man, whose malice brought grief upon the rulers.
sentences.s535-s5: A cry rose from behind: "O fortune — generosity! May your radiant head be exalted upon the throne of bounty."
sentences.s535-s6: Everyone continually said, "What a wonder this is!" — "From these people, one should take measure (learn a lesson)."
sentences.s535-s7: He came fleeing, his heart full of alarm; he kept rushing amid the ups and downs.
sentences.s535-s8: He saw a certain city, a large place; from that side a troop charged like wolves.
sentences.s535-s25: The charming one's words pleased him, and he made the secret manifest and revealed it openly.
sentences.s535-s26: “For I am Ardashir, a son/descendant of Sasan; I need a single pleasant piece of counsel,”
sentences.s535-s27: What shall we do with Karam and with Haftvād, whose name and lineage may not be in the world?
sentences.s535-s28: When the commander of Iran revealed the secret, his two young men both took up prayer.
sentences.s535-s29: Both said, “May a draught of evil always be far from you.”
sentences.s535-s30: May our body and soul be servants before you; may your spirit be ever-enduring.
sentences.s535-s31: You asked words of us; let us speak truly so that first you may prepare a remedy.
sentences.s535-s32: In battle with Kerm and Haftvād: if you are not sufficient, do not turn aside from justice.
sentences.s535-s21: When Alexander came in those days, he killed that evil king of the world.
sentences.s535-s22: They departed, and of them nothing remained except a foul name; nor did they attain the joyful paradise.
sentences.s535-s23: Not even this remained for Haftvād; it would come to the end (doom) of this ill-born race.
sentences.s535-s24: From their words the king's heart became as fresh as a flower in spring.
sentences.s535-s37: When Ardashir heard the words, he became wholly affectionate and pleasing to the heart.
sentences.s535-s38: He said to them thus: “It is fitting — their good and evil are (entrusted) with you and me.”
sentences.s535-s39: The youths answered him and adorned his discerning heart.
sentences.s535-s40: For we are your servants before you, ever at your feet, to be a guide to you in goodness.
sentences.s535-s9: When he entered into the narrow place he saw a house; at the door he saw two young strangers.
sentences.s535-s10: They stood for a while at the door; he inquired of him about these two, who were of pure mien.
sentences.s535-s11: “Who are you who, as of late, have come like this — where have you come from? You are dusty and dishevelled.”
sentences.s535-s12: He said to him, 'Ardashir passed by this side; we lagged behind him, astonished at the good.'
